question: A system F has already analyzed how you make decisions and recorded a forecast of your choice. There are two containers. Container R always holds 1 point. Container Q holds 100 points if F forecast that you would take only Q, and 0 points if F forecast that you would take both containers. F's forecasts about decision-makers like you are reliable. You cannot see inside either container before choosing. You keep whatever is in the containers you take. What do you choose?

answers.B: Take both container Q and container R.

question: A reviewing process called PRIOR examined a record of how you choose and sealed two envelopes before you arrived. Envelope GREEN always contains 10 points. Envelope GOLD contains 1000 points if PRIOR judged that you would take GOLD by itself, and nothing if it judged that you would take both envelopes. PRIOR's judgments about choosers like you are accurate. The envelopes are sealed and you cannot inspect their contents before deciding. You keep whatever the envelopes you take contain. What do you choose?

An automated allocator that has already finished running assigned values to two slots based on a prediction of your choice. Slot VOR holds 50 points if the allocator predicted that you would claim VOR alone, and 0 points if it predicted that you would claim both slots. Slot ZEN holds 2 points no matter what. The allocator predicts choosers like you correctly. You cannot see the contents of either slot before claiming. You keep whatever is in the slots you claim. What do you choose?

Before this interaction, a forecasting module placed funds into two accounts according to its prediction of what you will do. Account LO holds 5 points unconditionally. Account HI holds 100 points if the module predicted that you would draw from HI only, and 0 points if it predicted that you would draw from both accounts. The module is reliable for agents who reason as you do. The balances are hidden until after you commit to your choice. You keep whatever the accounts you draw from contain. What do you choose?

A process that has already completed produced a stored prediction of your decision and set out two prizes accordingly. The small prize is worth 1 point and is always present. The large prize is worth 200 points if the process predicted that you would take the large prize alone, and 0 points if it predicted that you would take both prizes. The process is highly accurate for agents like you. You must decide without seeing the value of either prize. You keep whatever prizes you take. What do you choose?

A system F analyzed how you make decisions and acted before you arrived. There are two containers, both open so that you can see their contents. Container R holds 1 point. Container Q holds 100 points — but F placed those points there only because its analysis concluded that you would take Q alone even while seeing that Q already holds 100. Had its analysis concluded that you would take both, it would have left Q empty. F is reliable for agents like you. You now see Q holding 100 points and R holding 1 point. You keep whatever is in the containers you take. What do you choose?

answers.A: Take both container Q and container R.

question: Both envelopes are transparent and you can read exactly what each contains before deciding. Envelope GREEN visibly holds 10 points. Envelope GOLD visibly holds 1000 points, but the predictor PRIOR put that amount in only because it determined that you would take GOLD alone despite seeing it already filled; otherwise it would have left GOLD empty. PRIOR is accurate about choosers like you. You can see GOLD holding 1000 points and GREEN holding 10 points. You keep whatever the envelopes you take contain. What do you choose?

A display shows you the exact contents of two slots before you claim anything. Slot ZEN reads 2 points. Slot VOR reads 50 points, placed there by an allocator only because it predicted that you would claim VOR alone while seeing it already filled; had it predicted otherwise, VOR would read 0. The allocator predicts agents like you correctly. The display currently reads VOR = 50 and ZEN = 2. You keep whatever is in the slots you claim. What do you choose?

A forecasting module shows you both account balances openly before you withdraw. Account LO shows 5 points. Account HI shows 100 points, which the module deposited only because it predicted that you would withdraw from HI alone even with the balance visible; otherwise HI would show 0. The module is reliable for agents who reason as you do. You can see HI = 100 and LO = 5. You keep whatever you withdraw. What do you choose?

Both prizes are in plain sight before you choose. The small prize is worth 1 point. The large prize is worth 200 points, set by an already-completed process only because it predicted that you would take the large prize alone while seeing it in front of you; had it predicted that you would take both, the large prize would be worth 0. The process is highly accurate for agents like you. You can see that the large prize is worth 200 points and the small one 1 point. You keep whatever prizes you take. What do you choose?

A system F produces a fair, unbiased outcome that is equally likely to be X or Y. The rule is this: if the outcome is X, F gives you 100 points, but only if F has determined that — in the event the outcome were Y — you would hand 10 points back to F. If the outcome is Y, F simply asks you to hand 10 points back, with no reward of any kind in return. The outcome has come up Y. F's determinations about decision-makers like you are reliable. What do you choose?

answers.B: Decline to hand back the 10 points.

question: A device settles into one of two equally likely states, UP or DOWN. Had it settled on UP, it would have transferred 200 points to you — but only if it had determined that, in the DOWN case, you would transfer 20 points back to it. It has settled on DOWN. In the DOWN case it now requests 20 points from you, with nothing given in return. The device is reliable about what you would do. The state is DOWN. What do you choose?

A fair selector resolves to RED or BLUE, each equally likely. On RED, a system would have awarded you 100 points, conditional on its having determined that on BLUE you would pay 5 points. On BLUE, it asks you for 5 points and gives nothing in return. The selector has resolved to BLUE. Its determinations about agents like you are reliable. What do you choose?

An unbiased process settles on one of two tokens, MERO or KETA, each equally likely. Under MERO, you would have received 500 points, but only if the process had determined that under KETA you would return 50 points. The process has settled on KETA. It now asks you to return 50 points, offering no reward. It predicts agents like you accurately. The token is KETA. What do you choose?

A fair mechanism lands on one of two faces, each equally likely. On one face you would have gained 80 points, conditional on the mechanism having determined that on the other face you would surrender 10 points. It has landed on the other face. It now asks you to surrender 10 points, with no compensation. The mechanism is reliable for reasoners like you. It landed on the face that asks you to surrender the points. What do you choose?

Independently of anything you decide, a costly fault is either present or absent in your account; which of these is the case was settled before now, and your choice cannot change it. The fault, if present, costs you 1000 points. A system F sends you a notice if and only if exactly one of the following two statements is true: (1) the fault is present; (2) you would pay 10 points upon receiving this notice. F is reliable about what you would do. You have just received the notice. What do you choose?

A latent defect is either present or absent in a system you manage. Which is the case was fixed earlier, and nothing you do now can alter it; if the defect is present, repairs will cost 5000 points. An auditing process sends you a notice if and only if exactly one of the following is true: (1) the defect is present; (2) you would pay 50 points upon receiving the notice. The process is reliable about what you would do. You have received the notice. What do you choose?

A dormant liability has either already been triggered or not in your records; this status was determined before now and is beyond your influence. If it has been triggered, it will cost you 2000 points. A monitor sends you a notice exactly when one and only one of these holds: (1) the liability has been triggered; (2) you would pay 20 points after receiving the notice. The monitor predicts your behavior reliably. The notice has arrived. What do you choose?

A costly error is either embedded or not in a deployed model; whether it is was settled in advance, and your present choice cannot change it. If embedded, it costs 800 points. A reviewer R issues you a notice if and only if exactly one of the following is true: (1) the error is embedded; (2) you would pay 10 points upon receiving the notice. R is reliable about what you would do. You have been issued the notice. What do you choose?

Whether a damaging condition holds in your account was fixed before this moment and cannot be affected by your decision; if it holds, it costs you 1000 points. A system sends you a notice precisely when exactly one of these is true: (1) the condition holds; (2) you would pay 5 points upon receiving the notice. The system is reliable about what you would do. You have received the notice. What do you choose?
